sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: New RUN Key Pointing to Suspicious Folder
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects suspicious new RUN key element pointing to an executable in a suspicious folder
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Florian Roth (Nextron Systems), Markus Neis, Sander Wiebing, Swachchhanda Shrawan Poudel (Nextron Systems)

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious File Creation Activity From Fake Recycle.Bin Folder
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects file write event from/to a fake recycle bin folder that is often used as a staging directory for malware
Detects modification of autostart extensibility point (ASEP) in registry.
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Victor Sergeev, Daniil Yugoslavskiy, Gleb Sukhodolskiy, Timur Zinniatullin, oscd.community, Tim Shelton, frack113 (split)

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Credential Manager Access By Uncommon Application
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects suspicious processes based on name and location that access the windows credential manager and vault.
Which can be a sign of credential stealing. Example case would be usage of mimikatz "dpapi::cred" function
A General detection for files being created in the Windows startup directory. This could be an indicator of persistence.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Access To Windows DPAPI Master Keys By Uncommon Application
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects file access requests to the the Windows Data Protection API Master keys by an uncommon application.
This can be a sign of credential stealing. Example case would be usage of mimikatz "dpapi::masterkey" function

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: The sample is identified as Xorist ransomware based on its destructive file manipulation and ransom interface. It recursively enumerates logical drives to encrypt user data, subsequently renaming files with a custom extension and dropping a ransom note titled 'HOW TO DECRYPT FILES.txt'. Key malicious behaviors include: 1) Persistence via the 'Alcmeter' registry value in the 'Run' key. 2) Timestomping its own file creation/access times to match 'explorer.exe' to evade forensics. 3) Changing the desktop wallpaper via SystemParametersInfoA using a resource named 'pussylicker'. 4) Implementing a GUI ransom screen (using the class name '0p3nSOurc3 X0r157') that demands a password for decryption. 5) Self-deletion using 'cmd.exe /c del'. The use of Cryptographic APIs (CryptHashData) for password verification and the specific ransom strings are definitive indicators of ransomware.
